---
title: "The Archetecture Of Profit In Irish Housing 0/7"
theme: "Irish Housing"
date: 2026-06-10
output:
  html:
    options: 
      number_sections: true
    meta:
      css: ["@default", "@article"]
      js: ["@copy-button" "@fold-details" "@callout" "@sidenotes", "@appendix"]
---

# The Architecture of Profit — Overview, Methodology, and How to Read This Series

*On the curious art of growing wealthy while standing still.*

## Abstract

Across the decade 2015–2024, the development sub-sector of the Irish construction industry, the firms that acquire land, secure planning permission, and sell sites — retained, on average, more than 80 cents of every euro of new economic value it created as gross operating surplus. In 2024, the figure was 76.3 cents; at its peak in 2017 it was 89.5. Over the decade, this sub-sector (NACE 411, "Development of building projects") accumulated approximately €8.167 billion in gross operating surplus. On the European Union's own comparative statistics, Ireland's developer value margin is the seventh highest of thirty-four countries, and on a per-capita basis the sector extracts roughly four times the EU average.

This report argues that this surplus isn’t, in the main, a return on construction, risk, or productive effort. It is a planning-permission rent: value created by the state's own zoning and permission decisions, captured privately because no mechanism exists to do otherwise. The argument is built entirely from the state's own published data, the Central Statistics Office's National Accounts, the same office's enterprise and land-price series, and Eurostat's Structural Business Statistics, and from the audited accounts and public announcements of the companies and agencies involved. Interpreting it takes no ideology, only arithmetic.

This piece sets out the framing, the method, and the map of the eight-part series that follows.

---

## A Tale of Two Quotations

> Roads are made, streets are made, railway services are improved, electric light turns night into day, electric trams glide swiftly to and fro, water is brought from reservoirs a hundred miles off in the mountains - and all the while the landlord sits still. Every one of those improvements is effected by the labour and cost of other people. Many of the most important are effected at the cost of the municipality and of the ratepayers. To not one of those improvements does the land monopolist, as a land monopolist, contribute, and yet by every one of them the value of his land is sensibly enhanced. He renders no service to the community, he contributes nothing to the general welfare; he contributes nothing even to the process from which his own enrichment is derived.
>
> &mdash; Winston Churchill, President of the Board of Trade, Budget Speech, Edinburgh, 17 July 1909

---

> The former Ford Motor Distribution site in Cork's South Docklands entered NAMA in 2008. Over the following decade, Cork City Council invested in surrounding public realm, the state funded Marina Park adjacent to the site, An Bord Pleanála granted planning permission for 1,178 apartments, and public infrastructure was laid throughout the docklands quarter. The land monopolist — in this instance, the developer who acquired the site from NAMA in 2018 for a reported €15 million — sat still. To not one of those improvements did the developer contribute as the owner of the land. Yet by every one of them, the value of the holding was enhanced. In February 2025, the state — through the Land Development Agency — purchased 337 of those apartments for €150 million. Ten times the original price of the entire site, for less than a third of its output. The landlord's enrichment: approximately €510 million in planning-permission uplift, retained in full. Tax captured on that uplift: zero.
>
> &mdash; Cork Docklands, 2018–2025. Sources: Irish Times, LDA press release, Glenveagh Properties plc Full Year Results 2025

---

Churchill made this observation in 1909 as a Liberal cabinet minister, defending a land value tax in Lloyd George's People's Budget. He was not, by any reasonable historical account, a communist. He was an imperialist, a conservative with liberal interludes, a man of profound and often catastrophic conviction about the natural order of things, and he understood, with perfect clarity, that landowners who grow wealthy by sitting still while others build around them aren’t producers of wealth but extractors of it. That this observation now reads as radical is a measure not of how far left the analysis has drifted, but of how far the Overton window of acceptable housing economics has been moved by those who benefit most from keeping it there.

This report doesn’t argue that the Irish housing crisis is caused by greed, corruption, or conspiracy. It argues something more uncomfortable: that it is caused by a system operating exactly as its incentive structure was designed to operate, sustained by a political and media environment in which the framing of the crisis (high costs, difficult planning, volatile markets) consistently serves the interests of those extracting value from it. The data that follows was compiled from primary government sources. It doesn’t require ideology to interpret. It requires only arithmetic.

---

## A Note on Methodology and Tools

### Artificial Intelligence

Claude (Anthropic, Claude Sonnet 4.6) was used throughout the preparation of this report as an analytical and drafting partner. All primary data was sourced directly by the author from official statistical releases: CSO National Accounts (CNAPA01), CSO Annual Enterprise Survey (BAA12), CSO Agricultural Land Prices (ARA02), CSO Residentially Zoned Land Prices (RZLPA01), and Eurostat Structural Business Statistics (sbs_ovw_act). All Eurostat data was downloaded directly from the Eurostat databrowser by the author and validated prior to inclusion.

AI was used to interpret, cross-reference, and draft analysis from that data, not to generate or substitute for primary sources. Every figure in this report is traceable to its cited primary source. Where data was unavailable, estimated, or back-calculated from published headline figures, this is explicitly noted in the relevant source footnote.

The analytical framework, the dual-source metric structure, the sectoral and cross-country comparisons, the counter-argument engagement, the case study selection, and the policy conclusions — was developed through iterative discussion between the author and the AI, with all methodological decisions made by the author. The report reflects the author's analysis and conclusions.

#### CSO Data Tool

CSO data exploration and retrieval for this report was supported by a
custom PxStat interface tool, which provides
accessible querying of the CSO\'s PxStat API --- Ireland\'s primary
statistical data platform. The tool makes CSO primary data more
accessible to researchers, journalists, and policymakers who may not
have the technical infrastructure to query the API directly.

The tool is open source and freely available at:

[CSO_datasette](https://github.com/CathalByrneGit/CSO_datasette)

I encourage its use for independent verification of the
figures in this report and for broader engagement with CSO primary data.
The CSO\'s PxStat platform holds the full range of National Accounts,
enterprise survey, price, and demographic data referenced throughout
this report. Making that data more accessible is a contribution to the
quality of public policy debate in Ireland  the same goal this report
pursues through its analysis.

> *All primary data in this report is publicly available and independently verifiable. The author invites scrutiny.*

---

## How to Read This Series

The full report runs to roughly 120,000 words of analysis and primary-source tables. It is published here as eight self-contained posts, which can be read in order or sampled individually. The argument is cumulative: each post establishes facts that the next builds on.

**Post 1 — 81 Cents: What Ireland's Developers Actually Keep.** The central finding. The dual-source framework (the two ways to measure profit, and why both matter), the full ten-year record of developer margins, the labour-versus-capital split, and the complete extraction profile, including the reinvestment rate, agency-worker reliance, and wage data that show what kind of business this is.

**Post 2 — Seventh in Europe, and Falling Further Behind.** The European context. How Ireland ranks against thirty-three other countries, the per-capita extraction rate, and the risk-adjusted comparison that tests whether the margins are compensation for genuine risk.

**Post 3 — The Permission and the Profit.** The mechanism. How planning permission converts agricultural land worth €24,000 an acre into residential land worth €643,000 an acre, why "developers pay high land prices" doesn’t explain the margins, and how vertical integration hides the profit between subsidiaries.

**Post 4 — 61 Representations a Year.** The politics. How a policy response that hasn’t worked for a decade is sustained, the lobbying, the policy fingerprints, the land taxes that died in implementation, and the state's presence on all four sides of the transaction.

**Post 5 — €15 Million In, €525 Million Out.** The case study. A single, fully documented transaction chain in Cork — every figure sourced to the developer's own stock-exchange filings, the government's own press releases, and the Land Development Agency's own announcements.

**Post 6 — Reaping Where They Never Sowed.** The theory. Why none of this is an accident, from Adam Smith and John Stuart Mill through the 2024 and 2025 Nobel laureates to the present, the intellectual tradition that has named this problem for two and a half centuries.

**Post 7 — The Oldest Idea in Economics.** The remedy. Five structural interventions, each with a fifty-year Irish pedigree, the constitutional record that says they are lawful, and the international examples that prove they work.

**Post 8 — The Data Was Always There.** The companion piece for researchers and journalists. Why no official body connected this data before, the statistical infrastructure that's missing, the open questions and unexplored avenues a better-resourced analytical effort could pursue (corporation tax effective rates, uncommenced permissions stock, the judicial review narrative, foreign-control share, the live RZLT test), and the full source registry for anyone who wishes to reproduce or extend the analysis.

---

*Every figure in this series is drawn from publicly available data and is independently verifiable. The author invites scrutiny, correction, and reuse.*
